article_title: Homelab projects to try this weekend (March 27 - 29)
core_thesis: This article presents three accessible, self-hosted homelab projects—ArchiveBox, Ghostfolio, and ntfy—that can be deployed over a weekend to enhance personal data archiving, financial privacy, and system monitoring. It emphasizes that homelabbing does not require expensive hardware; old computers running Linux are sufficient.
detailed_summary: The article, published on How-To Geek, offers three weekend homelab projects. First, ArchiveBox is a self-hosted web archiving tool that automatically backs up public portions of websites, social media, and media content. It requires a NAS for large-scale storage. Second, Ghostfolio is a privacy-focused investment portfolio tracker that provides analytics like ROAI and supports mobile access via a reverse proxy or Cloudflare Tunnel. Third, ntfy is a self-hosted push notification service that sends alerts for system events (e.g., backup failures, Docker updates, resource warnings) via REST API and mobile apps. The article also encourages beginners to repurpose old hardware with Linux to start a homelab without upfront cost.

entities.name: ArchiveBox
entities.role: Self-hosted web archiving tool for backing up public internet content.

entities.name: Ghostfolio
entities.role: Self-hosted investment portfolio tracker with privacy-focused analytics.

entities.name: ntfy
entities.role: Self-hosted push notification service for homelab monitoring.

entities.name: Patrick Campanale
entities.role: Author and technology writer with homelab expertise.

key_data_points: ArchiveBox can archive public Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, SoundCloud content, and entire websites, but only as static snapshots (no live database links). (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: can be used to preserve evidence or intelligence from public sources.)

Ghostfolio provides ROAI (return on average investment) metrics for today, week-to-date, month-to-date, and year-to-date, and can be accessed as a progressive web app behind a reverse proxy or Cloudflare Tunnel. (🟢 Relevant to personal finance: enables private portfolio tracking without third-party data exposure.)

ntfy works via PUT/POST HTTP requests to its REST API and integrates with iOS and Android apps, allowing custom alerts for backup status, Docker updates, disk space, CPU, and RAM usage. (🟢 Relevant to homelab/DevOps: provides a lightweight, self-hosted alternative to commercial monitoring services.)

The article states that a homelab can be started with any old computer running Linux, making it accessible even without new hardware investment. (🟢 Relevant to student budget: low-cost entry into homelabbing.)
methodology_steps: For ArchiveBox: Deploy ArchiveBox in a Docker container or directly on a Linux server. Configure it to archive specific URLs or public social media profiles. Ensure sufficient storage (preferably a NAS) for large archives. Set up a cron job or scheduler for automatic periodic archiving.

For Ghostfolio: Deploy Ghostfolio via Docker. Configure it to connect to your investment accounts (manual transaction entry or API if supported). Set up a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx) or Cloudflare Tunnel for secure remote access. Install the progressive web app on your phone for mobile monitoring.

For ntfy: Deploy ntfy server using Docker or direct installation. Configure the REST API endpoint. Write scripts (e.g., bash, Python) that send PUT/POST requests to ntfy on events like backup completion/failure, Docker container updates, or resource thresholds. Install the ntfy app on your phone and subscribe to the topic.
conclusions: ArchiveBox is a practical tool for preserving internet content locally, but its snapshot-only nature limits its use for dynamic sites. It is best suited for static content archiving and can be integrated into a homelab with a NAS for storage.
Ghostfolio offers a privacy-respecting alternative to commercial portfolio trackers, with mobile access via reverse proxy. It is ideal for users who want full control over their financial data.

ntfy provides a simple, self-hosted notification system that can be integrated into any homelab workflow, reducing silent failures and improving system awareness. Its REST API makes it highly customizable.
The article reinforces that homelabbing is accessible to anyone with old hardware, lowering the barrier to entry for students and hobbyists.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, offering three concrete, deployable projects that enhance system monitoring, data preservation, and financial privacy. As a programmer, you can extend ntfy with custom scripts for your own alerts, and ArchiveBox can be used for Red Teaming intelligence gathering. The emphasis on using old hardware aligns with a student budget, and the step-by-step deployment advice (Docker, reverse proxy) matches your technical skill level. The projects also provide practical experience with self-hosting and automation.
extra_advice: Consider combining ntfy with your existing monitoring stack (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana) to send alerts for security events like failed SSH logins or unusual network traffic. For ArchiveBox, automate archiving of threat actor blogs or CVE pages to build a personal threat intelligence repository.
